---
title: Stitches for Belarus
date: 2026-03-17
---
The piece of red and green cloth with an ornament was hung out all around during public holidays and on September 1st at school. Back then, I had no relationship with it, nor any opinion about its appearance. It was a decoration in the background, accompanying the performance of the anthem and minutes of silence. Perhaps in those moments it spoke to me‚ playing with the wind while everyone stood still. The collective gathering made the moment significant, asking for crisp shirts, combed hair, and endless flowers. The flag was signaling that I'm becoming a part of the adults' world.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

### What if Matrena were born in a different village?

Red and white pattern appeared on the BSSR flag in 1951. It was added to a scarlet communist standard, along with a slice of green. In the aftermath of WW2, Belarus joined the UN, and the national identity of the republic was later revealed in flag design. The ornament was next to identical to the present one, which stands out from the colour abstractions elsewhere in the bloc. It appears to be the only flag directly quoting cultural symbols. Even though it was a diplomatic necessity, rather than the initiative of the local government, Mikhail Katsar directed the group of artists to the folklore heritage. Since then, the ornament above has become a testament to the values of the soviet past.

The specimen quotes a design by Matreona Markevich from 1917. She crafted ceremonial cloth used in rural weddings and bread-and-salt rituals. Little has been written about Matrena's life, but it's clear that her husband lost his life for anti-Soviet propaganda, and the family was dekulakised. The original embroidered cloth did not survive ‚Äî it was likely confiscated by the NKVD or lost during the war. Mikhail Katsar, the ethnographer who appropriated the pattern, was Matreona's brother.

Ornamental patterns are rooted in culture, embodying meaning through generations. Matrena took her inspiration from local plants and flowers in the village of Kastsilishcha. On the other hand, researchers infer meaning by observing where patterns appear and how they are used. The central element with horn-like shapes is often interpreted as a cultivated field, a female principle, or fertile earth. A cross may represent sun, cosmic order, and directions of the world. Central composition and repeated motifs are associated with protection, hope, and fulfilment. And so on. Meaning in the making has been collective and intuitive, not explicit.

The identity of the ornament might be read through the past or assigned in the present. Meanwhile, the pattern's formal composition speaks a language of its own. From afar, it felt alien. But the more I look at the bar above, the less I remember about the trauma of the past. I'm seeing a lush field. Wind gently moves through flowers under the sun.
